description: Content Manifest (#1821): structural source of truth classifying every git-tracked top-level repository entry into one of four buckets. This is the Wave-1 shippability audit for the engine/content split (#1669): it converts the brittle installer denylist into an allowlist-by-classification and is the authoritative input that #1875 (the content/ move) consumes. Every top-level entry MUST appear exactly once; the deterministic gate `task verify:content-manifest` (packages/core/src/content-manifest/, exposed via `deft-ts verify-content-manifest`) fails CI when a top-level entry is unclassified, when a classified entry no longer exists, when a bucket value is invalid, or when a path is listed twice. See vBRIEF: vbrief/completed/2026-06-22-1821-content-manifest-v1-shippability-classification.vbrief.json. Refs #1821 (this story), #1669 (epic), #1875 (content/ move), #878 (practices/processes RFC -> #1874 content-doctrine umbrella).

buckets.label: Content
buckets.description: Framework knowledge/guidance/assets that a consumer consumes. In the #1669 model these live under a `content/` root and deposit into the consumer's `.deft/core/`: standards docs, skills, templates, packs, conventions prose, core/framework docs, and deposited scaffolds.

buckets.label: Engine
buckets.description: Executable framework runtime -- the code that enforces and runs deft. In the #1669 model this ships as the CLI package (npm/pip), not as editable deposited content: the TS packages, the Go installer, the Python scripts, the Taskfile + task fragments, the `run` CLI launchers, and the deposited git hooks.

buckets.label: Harness
buckets.description: Build / test / language toolchain configuration required to build and test the engine inside THIS repo. Not shipped as-is; needed to produce the engine + content artifacts: package/lockfiles, compiler/linter/test config, and the framework's own test suite.

buckets.label: Repo-dev
buckets.description: Maintainer-only / repository artifacts that are neither shippable content nor engine runtime: CI config, git plumbing, planning docs, history/incident logs, the maintainer's own vBRIEF lifecycle, and the deft project's own product/release docs.

straddlePolicy: C3 straddle policy (#1669 Wave-1 LockedDecisions): the manifest classifies at top-level granularity -- one bucket per top-level entry. When a top-level entry contains a minority of files belonging to a different bucket (a 'straddle'), classify the entry by its DOMINANT purpose, set `straddle: true`, and record the minority content in `note`. Straddles are resolved to finer granularity only when the entry is physically split (e.g. during the #1875 content/ move). The gate does NOT descend into directories; it validates the top-level set only.

entries.note: Installer-managed deposit of the deft skills (.agents/skills/) referenced by AGENTS.md; consumed by agents at runtime.
Git attributes (linguist/vendored markers, EOL); repository plumbing.
Deposited pre-commit/pre-push hooks (#1463) that invoke the gate scripts -- executable enforcement installed by `task setup` / the installer.
GitHub Actions workflows, issue/PR templates, CODEOWNERS; maintainer CI only.
Git ignore rules; repository plumbing.
Node version pin used to build the TS engine.
Maintainer planning artifacts (e.g. generated codebase MAP projection); never ships.
Installer-managed framework guidance entry; the managed-section is rendered from templates/agents-entry.md and deposited to consumers.
Maintainer changelog; promoted into GitHub release bodies at release time.
Maintainer contributor guide for the deft repo itself.
Travels with the deposited payload; the engine CLI package also bundles its own copy at publish time.
The deft project's own product requirements doc; not consumer framework content.
The deft project's own project doc (deprecation redirect / generated export); not consumer content.
Consumer-facing onboarding doc; the AGENTS.md managed-section points consumers at .deft/core/QUICK-START.md for idempotent refresh.
GitHub-facing repository readme for the deft project.
Repo-level references index for maintainers.
The deft project's own roadmap; updated at release time.
Top-level deft skill entry (the `deft` skill); part of the shipped skills surface.
The deft project's own generated specification export / deprecation redirect; not consumer content.
Root task runner manifest that orchestrates the engine gates and lifecycle verbs.
Consumer-facing upgrade guide cited by the doctor and install surfaces.

Go installer (cmd/deft-install) -- the canonical bootstrap/upgrade binary.
Coding standards docs (coding.md, hygiene.md, testing.md, build-output.md, toolchain.md).
Framework commands reference doc.
Context-engineering standards docs.
Framework contract docs (boundary-maps, deterministic-questions, hierarchy).
Conventions prose docs ship as content; co-located structured data files (rule-ownership.json, content-manifest.json) are engine-consumed gate inputs that travel with the same directory.
Core framework docs (glossary.md, project.md, ralph.md, versioning.md).
Per-platform deployment guidance docs (aws, azure, gcp, fly-io, cloudflare, ...).
Predominantly maintainer/repo docs (FILES.md, RELEASING.md, analysis/); a minority of consumer-facing concept docs (CONCEPTS.md, BROWNFIELD.md) are co-located and split to finer granularity later.

Per-language standards docs (python, go, typescript, rust, ...).
The full framework guidelines -- the canonical entry doc consumers read.
Meta docs including lessons.md (projected from the lessons content pack).
Root pnpm workspace manifest for the TS engine build/test.
TypeScript engine monorepo (@deftai/cli, @deftai/core, @deftai/types) -- the gate + lifecycle runtime.
Versioned content packs (lessons, etc.) loaded via the packs:slice surface.
Pattern docs (goal-gate-determinism, build-model-ahead, generator-verifier-loop, ...).
Platform-specific guidance docs (unity, 2600, ...).
pnpm dependency lockfile for the TS engine.
pnpm workspace package globs for the TS engine.
Python project + pytest/ruff/mypy config for the Python engine scripts and their tests.
Reference docs (cost-models, ip-risk, plain-english-ux, ...).

Source-control standards docs (git.md, github.md).

Strategy docs (delegate, interview, ...).
